attributes.stem:
A previously well 16-year-old boy presents with a 10 day history of increasing pain in his lower abdomen, maximal on the right side. The pain is constant in nature and is now so bad that he cannot go to school.

His blood pressure is 115/70 mmHg, pulse 98/min and temperature 37.8C. On abdominal examination there is a mass palpable in the right iliac fossa which is tender on palpation. The rest of the physical examination is unremarkable.  A CT scan is performed (Image).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate plan of management?
attributes.explanation:
The finding of a palpable tender mass in the right iliac fossa that has been present for more than three days is characteristic of an appendix mass. This is the result of a perforation of the appendix which has become sealed off. The consequences of such a perforation can vary from an uncomplicated inflammatory mass to abscess formation. In this case the clinical diagnosis has been confirmed by the CT scan results. The CT shows a clearly defined 8 cm heterogenous mass in the right iliac fossa. This is an appendix abscess. An appendiceal abscess is found in about 4% of patients diagnosed with appendicitis. Although it is relatively uncommon, it is important to be aware of the implications in finding an appendiceal abscess and how it affects the patient's management plan.

The current recommended management for an appendix abscess is a non-surgical approach. For an appendiceal abscess >5cm, percutaneous image-guided drainage is the most appropriate initial plan of management. This is the most appropriate treatment plan for the patient described in this clinical scenario. If there is no response to percutaneous drainage, then a laparoscopic or open appendicectomy with a washout and drainage should be performed.

For cases of a phlegmon (uncomplicated inflammatory mass with no abscess formation) or an abscess <5cm, it is recommended that the patient is treated with antibiotics and conservative management. This approach is associated with more than 90% success rate, and shorter hospital stay. It is important to consider that in older patients other underlying and potentially more sinister conditions must be considered. In such cases, if nonoperative management is successful, then in six to eight weeks, if not previously had a recent colonoscopy, the patient should be offered a colonoscopy as the risk of an underlying caecal or appendiceal neoplasm can be significant.

The appendiceal tumour rate in patients who have presented with an appendix abscess is at least 10% and this particular occurs in individuals over the age of 35. Thus all patients who have had an appendix abscess should be considered for a colonoscopy and an elective appendicectomy.

A surgical approach (laparoscopic drainage and appendicectomy or open appendicectomy) is not the appropriate treatment for a large (4 - 6 cm) appendiceal abscess. Immediate surgery on an appendiceal abscess is associated with a significant increase in morbidity. This is due to the changes in the anatomy of the appendix and the surrounding tissues during acute inflammation, making any surgical approach be technically demanding. Emergency surgical intervention would only be indicated if the patient's condition was deteriorating, with on-going sepsis or perforation of the abscess.
learningPoints: An appendiceal abscess should be treated with antibiotics and/or percutaneous drainage, depending on the size of the abscess.
Immediate surgical treatment of an appendix abscess can be associated with a significant increase in patients' morbidity.
references:
Andersson RE, Petzold MG. Nonsurgical Treatment of Appendiceal Abscess or
Phlegmon. Ann Surg 2007;246: 741–48. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17968164/

Forsyth J, Lasithiotakis K, Peter M. The evolving management of the appendix mass in the era of laparoscopy and interventional radiology. The Surgeon 2017; 15 109-15. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27612947/
Salminen R, Alajaaski J, Rautio T, et al. Appendiceal Tumor Prevalence in Patients With Periappendicular Abscess. JAMA Surg. 2025; 160:526-34.
Diaz JJ, Ceresoli M, Herron T, et al. Current management of acute appendicitis in adults: What you need to know. J Trauma Acute Care Surg. 2025; 98: 181–189.
